title: ChatGPT for Seniors
subtitle: A gentle, step-by-step guide to using ChatGPT for everyday life — no tech experience needed.

modules.title: What is ChatGPT and How to Get Started
lessons: ChatGPT is a free computer programme you can use in your web browser — there is nothing to download or install. You simply go to chat.openai.com, create a free account with your email address, and you can start typing questions or requests in plain English, just like sending a text message. It is available on any device including tablets and smartphones, and the text on screen can usually be made larger using your browser settings. Many people are surprised by how simple it is once they try it for the first time.

ChatGPT can help you with a wide range of everyday tasks: answering questions, explaining things in simple language, writing letters and messages, helping you plan your week, or even just having a conversation when you want to think something through. It cannot browse the internet by default, make phone calls, or access your personal accounts — it only knows what you type to it in the conversation. It is important to understand that it is a very capable assistant, but it is not a person, and it can sometimes make mistakes, so it is always worth double-checking important information.

Staying safe with ChatGPT is straightforward as long as you follow a few simple rules. Never share personal information like your home address, bank details, passwords, or National Insurance number in your conversations. ChatGPT does not need any of this information to help you, and if any website or person tells you that you must share such details to use AI, that is a scam. The legitimate ChatGPT website is chat.openai.com — be careful of imitation websites with similar names.
modules.mini_task: Open chat.openai.com on your computer or tablet. If you do not have an account, create one with your email. Then type this question exactly: "I am new to ChatGPT. Can you suggest 5 simple things I could ask you to help me with today?" Read the suggestions and try one that interests you.

modules.title: Practical Uses Every Day
lessons: One of the most useful things ChatGPT can do for you is answer health-related questions in plain, easy-to-understand language. If your doctor uses a medical term you do not recognise, you can type it into ChatGPT and ask: "What does atrial fibrillation mean in simple terms, and what are the common symptoms?" ChatGPT will explain it clearly without jargon. It is important to remember that ChatGPT is not a doctor and its answers are general information — always follow the advice of your actual medical team for personal health decisions.

ChatGPT is brilliant for helping you write letters, cards, and messages. If you need to write a formal letter to your bank, a complaint to a company, or even a heartfelt birthday message for a grandchild, just describe the situation and ChatGPT will write a draft for you. For example, type: "Help me write a polite letter to my landlord asking them to fix a leak in the kitchen — I want to be friendly but clear." You can then copy the draft, change any details, and use it yourself. It saves the stress of staring at a blank page.

Recipes and cooking help are another area where ChatGPT genuinely shines for everyday life. If you have a few ingredients in the fridge and do not know what to make, type: "I have chicken, rice, onions, and tomatoes. What simple meal can I make for two people?" ChatGPT will suggest a recipe with clear step-by-step instructions. You can ask it to simplify steps, convert measurements from cups to grams, or suggest a substitution if you are missing an ingredient. It is like having a patient, knowledgeable friend in the kitchen.
modules.mini_task: Think of a letter or message you have been meaning to write — to a friend, a company, or a family member. Type into ChatGPT: "Please help me write a [short/formal/friendly] [letter/message/card] to [describe who and why]." Read the draft it produces and make any changes you like. If you are happy with it, use it!

modules.title: Staying Safe and Building Confidence
lessons: One of the most important skills when using AI is knowing that it can make mistakes — and knowing how to spot them. ChatGPT sometimes presents incorrect information confidently, which is why you should always verify anything important, especially medical, legal, or financial information, with a qualified professional or a trusted source like the NHS or a government website. If an answer seems surprising, unusual, or too good to be true, it is worth checking. Getting into the habit of asking "Are you certain about this?" can also prompt ChatGPT to acknowledge if it is less sure.

Privacy is important when using any digital tool, and ChatGPT is no exception. OpenAI uses conversations to improve its AI systems, so avoid typing anything you would not want others to potentially see — this includes your full name combined with sensitive personal details, private family matters, or confidential work information. You can go into ChatGPT's settings and turn off "Improve the model for everyone" if you prefer your conversations not to be used for training. The setting is in your profile menu under "Data controls."

Building confidence with ChatGPT is simply a matter of practice and patience. Start with small, low-stakes tasks — asking for a recipe, getting a word defined, or having something explained — before moving on to more complex requests like writing letters or planning holidays. If you do not like an answer, just type "That is not quite right — can you try again with more detail?" or "Can you explain that more simply?" ChatGPT will not judge you for asking the same thing multiple times, and it has infinite patience. The more you use it, the more natural it will feel.
modules.mini_task: Ask ChatGPT: "What should I never share with you to keep myself safe?" Read its answer and check it matches what you learned in this lesson. Then ask: "What are 3 simple things a beginner should practise with you this week?" Write down one idea from its answer that you will try today.

final_project: Over the next week, use ChatGPT to complete three real tasks from your own life — for example: write a letter to a company, get a recipe for a meal you want to try, and ask it to explain something you have always been curious about. At the end, write a few sentences (you can even ask ChatGPT to help you write them) about what surprised you most and what you found most useful.
